chunk_summary: This section discusses integrating clinic counseling with ICDS, ASHA, POSHAN, and NRC networks to improve infant and young child feeding under 2 years, emphasizing program touchpoints, referral pathways, and culturally effective strategies.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ICDS, ASHA, and POSHAN in supporting infant and young child feeding under 2 years.
Learn how to use IYCF indicators to track feeding status and growth outcomes.
Identify referral pathways for malnourished children and coordinate with NRCs and high-risk clinics.
Explore culturally effective counseling methods for low-literacy families and working mothers.
key_facts: Core IYCF indicators include early initiation, exclusive breastfeeding (EBF) under 6 months, timely complementary feeding at 6 months, minimum meal frequency (MMF), minimum dietary diversity (MDD), and minimum acceptable diet (MAD).
Anganwadi workers under ICDS provide growth monitoring and take-home rations for children aged 6–36 months.
ASHA workers reinforce breastfeeding, complementary feeding, and iron adherence during HBNC and HBYC home visits.
Digital tools like POSHAN Tracker can monitor growth data and trigger early intervention for faltering weight.
Children with wasting, edema, or repeated weight loss should be referred to Nutrition Rehabilitation Centers (NRCs).
Vitamin A supplementation schedules are tracked via child health cards and delivered through Anganwadi outreach sessions.
reference_quote: Under ICDS, Anganwadi workers provide growth monitoring and take-home rations for 6–36 months; counsel caregivers on correct preparation so rations are not diluted into low-calorie gruels. (Reference 3)
document_key_topics: Infant and young child feeding indicators
Integration of ICDS, ASHA, and POSHAN programs
